coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on chiral europium-doped carbon dots for specific and portable detection of tetracycline
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Min
Wei, Shanshan
Xie, Yuxin
Su, Kaihua
Yin, Xiangyu
Song, Xuewei
Hu, Kaixin
Sun, Guoying
Liu, Yibing
coredata.dc:description: The persistent residual of tetracycline (TC) in animal-derived food has posed a significant threat to human health, so the sensitive and specific detection of TC has extraordinary significance for food quality evaluation. However, most current methods were inevitably interfered by the highly similar structures of TCs family. To improve the specificity of TC detection, a novel ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on chiral europium-doped carbon dots (Eu-L/D-CDs) was designed for the sensing of TC. The blue fluorescence of Eu-L/D-CDs could be reduced by TC through static quenching (SQE) and internal filtering effects (IFE), while the red fluorescence could be enhanced through antenna effect (AE). The sensor achieved the rapid and sensitive detection of TC through fluorescence and colorimetric methods with detection limits as low as 2.82 nM and 3.74 nM, respectively. Importantly, the Eu-L-CDs-loaded test strips and hydrogel microspheres achieved the portable and visual detection of TC with the assistance of smartphone sensing platform. Additionally, the sensor could rapidly screen TC adjuvants in bacteria, providing a new approach to enhance the sensitivity of resistant bacteria to drugs. Therefore, this sensor not only proposed new insights into the sensitive and portable monitoring of TC, but also expanded the application of chiral CDs in the fields of biomedical sensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chiral carbon dots
Ratiometric fluorescence sensor
Smartphone sensing platform
Tetracycline
Animal-derived food

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric detection of TC
Specificity and anti-interference of the sensor
Adjuvants screening for TC resistant bacteria

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Jilin Province Natural Science Foundation (20220101350JC).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of Eu-L-CDs (inset: corresponding HR-TEM image and the size histogram distribution), (b) FT-IR spectra and (c) XPS full spectra of Eu-L-CDs. The high-resolution spectra of (d) C1s, (e) N1s, (f) O1s and (g) Eu3d of Eu-L-CDs. CD spectra of (h) L/D-TA and (i) Eu-L/D-CDs.

(a)Absorption of Eu-L-CDs, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra. (Inset：image of Eu-L-CDs under the sunlight (left) and 365 nm ultraviolet lamp irradiation (right). (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of Eu-L-CDs solution under different excitation wavelengths. (c) Absorption spectra of TC, Eu-L-CDs and Eu-L-CDs+TC solution. (d) Fluorescence lifetime decay curves of Eu-L-CDs and Eu-L-CDs+TC systems at 410 nm. (e) Fluorescence lifetime decay curves of Eu-L-CDs+5 μM TC and Eu-L-CDs+10 μM TC systems at 618 nm. (f) Mechanism diagram of TC enhanced Eu3+ emission.

(a) Fluorescence response and (b) liner plot of fluorescence intensity ratio F618/F410 in the presence of TC (c) CIE 1931 chromaticity coordinates. (d) Absorption spectra and (e) liner plot of absorbance at 390 nm vs the concentration of TC. (f) Schematic diagram of blue fluorescence quenching and red fluorescence enhancement process in the presence of TC.

The selectivity and anti-interference properties of Eu-L-CDs and Eu-L-CDs+TC systems against various possible (a) anions and cations and (b) amino acids and antibiotics. (Error bars represented the standard deviations，which were calculated from three sets of data for the same sample.).
The schematic illustration of Eu-L-CDs with the assistance of smartphone for the portable and visual detection of TC.

(a) The mechanism diagram of Eu-L-CDs for screening TC adjuvants. (b) TC concentration in bacteria after incubation with different content of TC assayed with Eu-L-CDs. (c) After incubating with TC, MET and Glu, the level of TC in bacteria.
Preparation of ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on Eu-L-CDs and the sensing procedure for TC.
Recovery of TC in actual samples by ratiometric fluorescence method.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on chiral europium-doped carbon dots for specific and portable detection of tetracycline

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Changchun University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, P R China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, P R China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Changchun University of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The persistent residual of tetracycline (TC) in animal-derived food has posed a significant threat to human health, so the sensitive and specific detection of TC has extraordinary significance for food quality evaluation. However, most current methods were inevitably interfered by the highly similar structures of TCs family. To improve the specificity of TC detection, a novel ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on chiral europium-doped carbon dots (Eu-L/D-CDs) was designed for the sensing of TC. The blue fluorescence of Eu-L/D-CDs could be reduced by TC through static quenching (SQE) and internal filtering effects (IFE), while the red fluorescence could be enhanced through antenna effect (AE). The sensor achieved the rapid and sensitive detection of TC through fluorescence and colorimetric methods with detection limits as low as 2.82 nM and 3.74 nM, respectively. Importantly, the Eu-L-CDs-loaded test strips and hydrogel microspheres achieved the portable and visual detection of TC with the assistance of smartphone sensing platform. Additionally, the sensor could rapidly screen TC adjuvants in bacteria, providing a new approach to enhance the sensitivity of resistant bacteria to drugs. Therefore, this sensor not only proposed new insights into the sensitive and portable monitoring of TC, but also expanded the application of chiral CDs in the fields of biomedical sensing.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline (TC) is the most widely used antibiotic in clinic and livestock farming owing to its effective antibacterial properties and high oral bioavailability [1,2]. However, TC can persist in food products, human body and environment for a long time due to its high stability and difficult degradability, inducing irreversible harm to humans and the ecological environment [3–5]. Research has shown that long-term intake of TC from food can induce the occurrence of diseases [6], such as gastrointestinal dysfunction, impaired bone development, fetal abnormalities, liver and kidney damage, metabolic disorders, and even cancer [7]. In addition, the long-term residue of TC is harmful to human immune system, resulting in the emergence of super resistant bacteria [8]. According to literature reports, an effective strategy to combat antibiotic resistant bacteria is to detect antibiotics in animal-derived foods at the initial stage [9]. Obviously, the development of a highly sensitive and specific sensor to monitor trace TC content has extraordinary significance in the field of food safety.

Currently, various methods for detecting TC have been established, including chemiluminescence immunoassay [10], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry [11], electrochemical analysis [12], fluorometry [13] and colorimetry [14] etc. Among them, fluorimetry has the advantages of easy operation, rapid response, high sensitivity, which has been applied in the field of analytical detection [15]. However, most single emission fluorescence probes are susceptible to interference from probe concentration and environmental fluctuations, making it difficult to detect target substance in complex samples [16]. To overcome this disadvantage, some ratiometric fluorescence probes have been designed to eliminate fluorescence fluctuations and allow for self-calibration to improve the accuracy of the detection systems [17–19]. In addition, ratiometric fluorescence sensors can produce significant color changes, which is beneficial for achieving portable and visual detection of target substances with the assistance of smartphone platform [20–24].

Chiral materials have obtained many admirable achievements in recognition sensing, biological imaging, drug delivery, medical diagnosis, optical technology [25–28]. The novel nanomaterial carbon dots (CDs) have been widely used in the fields of analytical detection due to their simple synthesis method, unique optical properties, excellent water solubility, and high biosafety [29,30]. The sensing probes based on chiral CDs not only have the advantages of high sensitivity and fast response, but also can achieve specific detection through precise three-dimensional structure of chiral CDs [28]. For example, Chen et al. designed a novel chiral fluorescence probe based on carbon dots-copper(II) system for ratiometric fluorescence detection of gemifloxacin [28]. Fan et al. prepared Eu-CDs as ratiometric fluorescence sensor to detect oxytetracycline (OTC), doxycycline (DOX), chlortetracycline (CTC) and TC [15]. Unfortunately, compared with the widespread attention on achiral CDs, the research on chiral ratiometric fluorescence CDs has seldom been reported. Additionally, most achiral probes to detect TC were inevitably interfered by the highly similar structures of the TCs family, which limited their practicality for the high-sensitivity and specificity detection of TC. Therefore, it still remains challenging to develop novel ratiometric fluorescence sensor to realize sensitive and portable detection of TC.

In this work, chiral Eu-L/D-CDs were prepared as a novel ratiometric fluorescence sensor for sensitive and portable detection of TC. As showed in Scheme 1, the blue fluorescence of Eu-L-CDs at 410 nm was specifically quenched by TC through internal filtering effect (IFE) and static quenching effect (SQE), while the red fluorescence at 618 nm was enhanced through antenna effect (AE). Significantly, Eu-L-CDs-loaded test strips and hydrogel microspheres were successfully applied for portable and visual analysis of TC with the assistance of a smartphone sensing platform. Additionally, the current sensor possessed lower LOD than most of methods and could achieve the evaluation of TC in pork kidney, eggs and milk through ratiometric fluorescence method, colorimetric method and solid sensors with satisfactory recovery rate. Therefore, the obtained sensor not only exhibited outstanding application potential in the field of recognition and sensing but also provided strong support for food quality detection.

L/D-tartaric acid (5 mmol), urea (5 mmol) and Eu(NO3)3·6 H2O (3 mmol) were dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water. Subsequently, the solution was transferred into a 50 mL teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 ℃ for 8 h. After naturally dropping to room temperature, the obtained solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm pore size membrane to get rid of the carbonized insoluble substances, and the filtrate was dialyzed with a 1000 Da membrane for 6 h to obtain a yellow Eu-L/D-CDs solution. Ultimately, the solution was dried by lyophilization to get Eu-L/D-CDs solid powder and then redissolved in ultrapure water for the following experiments. Additionally, the undoped L-CDs (only L-tartaric acid and urea) and achiral CDs (racemic tartaric acid, urea and Eu(NO3)3·6 H2O) were prepared under the same conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric detection of TC
ce:section.ce:para: 2.9 mL of different concentrations of TC (0.0–13.3 μM) were added into 100 μL of 1.0 mg·mL−1 Eu-L-CDs solution. After 1 min of interaction at room temperature, the fluorescence emission spectra and UV–vis absorption spectra of the Eu-L-CDs+TC mixed solutions were recorded for the quantitative analysis of TC.

The susceptible TC pollution samples of porcine kidney, milk, and eggs were chosen to assess the accuracy and practicality of the sensor for TC detection. The actual samples were brought from a local store and pretreated according to a routine procedure with slight modifications [6]. 1 % (v/v) trichloroacetic acid was employed to get rid of the proteins and lipids in these samples. Subsequently, they were sonicated for 20 min, then centrifuged and filtered to obtain the actual sample solutions. The obtained solutions were adjusted to neutral with NaOH solution and diluted 10 times for the following experiment.

The Eu-L-CDs-loaded test strips and hydrogel microspheres solid state sensors were prepared. The test strips were obtained by soaking them in the Eu-L-CDs solution for 30 min and letting them to be air-dried [31]. Afterwards, the obtained test strips were immersed in TC solutions with concentrations range from 0.0 μM to 13.3 μM for 30 min. To prepare alginate hydrogel microspheres, 50 mL of 2 % alginate solution was produced by heating and stirring in a constant temperature water bath at 60 ℃ for 30 min [32]. 50 mg of Eu-L-CDs powder was mixed and stirred evenly with the alginate solution. Afterward, the obtained solution was drawn into a 1 mL of injector and then it was dropped into 0.1 M of CaCl2 solution at a uniform flow rate to produce particles of appropriate diameter. Particles with an average diameter of 5.0 ± 0.1 mm were chosen to investigate the response of hydrogel microspheres to TC. The hydrogel microspheres were immersed in different concentrations of TC for 10 min. Afterward, the solid sensors were placed within the 365 nm dark box UV–vis analyzer and the smartphone was located at a fixed position. The camera software of smartphone was employed to capture fluorescent images, and the color recognition software (Color Picker) was performed to recognize fluorescent colors and convert them into RGB digital information. The experiment was repeated three times to satisfy the accuracy of smartphone assisted solid-state sensors detection of TC.

The TC resistant bacterium Escherichia coli was selected to screen TC effective adjuvants [33]. 2 mL of bacterial solution was centrifuged and washed with phosphate buffered saline (PBS) to get rid of the metabolites. The obtained solution was mixed and co-incubated with 100 μM TC and different concentrations (25, 50 and 100 mM) of MET/Glu at 37 ℃ for 1 h. Thereafter, the solution was washed by PBS for three times to remove the superfluous drugs, then redistributed in 3 mL of ultrapure water. The obtained solution was sonicated to lyse the bacteria and release TC, and then centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min to obtain the test solutions, which was measured through the sensor to quantitatively analyze TC in the bacteria.

After a detailed optimization procedure (Figs. S1–3), the Eu-L/D-CDs with optimal performance were successfully prepared using a one-step hydrothermal method. The Eu-L-CDs and Eu-D-CDs exhibited different chiral signals and detection performance. Compared with Eu-D-CDs, Eu-L-CDs exhibited better sensitivity for TC detection and storage stability, so Eu-L-CDs were selected for subsequent characterization and application [27,31,34].

The Eu-L-CDs displayed spherical structure with an average diameter of 1.83 ± 0.01 nm ( Fig. 1a). The conspicuous lattice spacing was 0.26 nm, corresponding to the (002) plane of the graphene carbon core (inset in Fig. 1a). The surface composition and structure of Eu-L-CDs were characterized by FT-IR spectra. As showed in Fig. 1b, a broad and strong peak around 3460 cm−1 corresponded to N-H and O-H stretching vibrations [35]. The peaks at 1740 cm−1 and 1380 cm−1 associated with CO and COOH stretching vibrations, respectively. The peak at 1140 cm−1 was originated from the stretching vibration of C-N bonds [31,36,37]. Therefore, the FT-IR spectra evidence demonstrated that the polar functional groups including -OH, -COOH, -CO and -NH were on the surface of Eu-L-CDs, which contributed to the excellent water solubility of Eu-L-CDs. In addition, Raman spectra exhibited two signal bands of D and G bands at 1282.69 and 1593.32 (Fig. S12b), indicating the presence of sp3 and sp2 carbon hybridization in Eu-L-CDs.

The functional groups and elemental composition on the surface of Eu-L-CDs were further researched with X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS). The peaks of XPS full spectra at 285.00 eV, 401.00 eV, 532.00 eV, and 1135.00 eV corresponded to C1s, N1s, O1s and Eu3d elements, and the percentage of each element was 74.59 %, 22.89 %, 2.50 % and 0.02 %, respectively (Fig. 1c). The high-resolution C1s spectra confirmed the existence of C-C (284.20 eV), C-N (285.80 eV) and COOH (288.45 eV) on the Eu-L-CDs surface (Fig. 1d) [38]. The high-resolved N1s spectra manifested the existence of C-N (399.10 eV) and N-H (401.15 eV) on the Eu-L-CDs surface (Fig. 1e) [39–41]. The fitted O1s spectra demonstrated the surface of Eu-L-CDs presented CO (531.10 eV) C-O (532.80 eV) and Eu-O (534.20 eV) groups (Fig. 1f) [36,42]. The fitted Eu3d spectra indicated that Eu3+ had been successfully doped in the Eu-L-CDs (Fig. 1g) [43]. The XPS spectra further supported the results of FT-IR spectroscopy, which verified the successful preparation of Eu-L-CDs.

The optical properties of Eu-L-CDs were studied by fluorescence excitation spectra, emission spectra and UV–vis absorption spectra. Two distinct absorption peaks were observed at 270 nm and 320 nm ( Fig. 2a, pink line), which was attributed to the n→π* electronic transition of CO bond and the π→π* electronic transition of CC bond, respectively [44]. The Eu-L-CDs presented faint yellow color under sunlight and brilliant blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp (inset in Fig. 2a). And the fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY) of Eu-L-CDs was as high as 8.35 % (Fig. S13). When the excitation wavelength increased from 305 nm to 385 nm, the emission peak gradually shifted from 410 nm to 450 nm (Fig. 2b). This phenomenon indicated that Eu-L-CDs exhibited typical excitation dependent emission with a conspicuous red shift in emission wavelength. The reason was that the surface defects on Eu-L-CDs captured excitons and generated new energy gaps [44]. The maximum emission peak at 410 nm was gained when the excitation wavelength was 325 nm.

As displayed in Fig. 1h, the CD signals of L-TA and D-TA located near 216 nm, while the CD signals of Eu-L-CDs and Eu-D-CDs were symmetrical near 226 nm (Fig. 1i), which revealed that Eu-L/D-CDs did not inherit the inherent chirality of the precursor. The formed new chiral structure was related to the π→π * transition of the sp2 hybrid carbon network [41]. After the addition of TC, two new signals appeared in the CD spectra of Eu-L/D-CDs+TC system at 263 nm and 293 nm, which were related to the chiral structures of TC (Fig. S4). The Eu-L/D-CDs exhibited similar fluorescence intensity under 325 nm excitation and the same absorption peak position in the UV–vis spectra, indicating that the prepared Eu-L/D-CDs possessed similar optical properties (Fig. S5). However, Eu-L-CDs exhibited better sensitivity than Eu-D-CDs (Fig. S6), so Eu-L-CDs was selected to sensitively and portably detect TC.

The fluorescence performances of Eu-L-CDs and Eu-L-CDs+TC in various environments were evaluated for practical applications. The fluorescence intensity of Eu-L-CDs and Eu-L-CDs+TC solutions did not fluctuate in different concentration of NaCl and H2O2 solution, as well as the irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. S7-S9). The fluorescence signals of Eu-L-CDs and Eu-L-CDs+TC solutions maintained stable in the pH range of 6.0–9.0, but they were unstable in acidic or alkali environments due to the protonation and deprotonation of the functional groups (Fig. S10). The maximum fluorescence intensity of Eu-L-CDs was observed at physiological environment pH of 7.0. Additionally, after 15 days of storage under room temperature, the fluorescence intensity of Eu-L-CDs at 410 nm could remain above 90 %, and the fluorescence intensity of Eu-D-CDs remained above 75 % (Fig. S11). The Eu-L-CDs remained fantastic optical properties, indicating its suitability for detection of TC in complex samples.

The response of Eu-L-CDs to TC could rapidly complete within 1 min and remain stable for 60 min, indicating a highly stable complex formed (Fig. S12). As represented in Fig. 3a, the L-CDs fluorescence intensity at 410 nm decreased, while the Eu3+ fluorescence intensity at 618 nm increased upon the addition of TC. An excellent linear correlation F618/F410 = 0.08404 CTC + 0.00408 (R2 = 0.999) between F618/F410 and TC was obtained from 0.03 μM to 13.30 μM, and the limit of detection (LOD) was 2.82 nM calculated according to the equation (LOD = 3δ/k). Furthermore, the UV–vis absorbance of Eu-L-CDs+TC system at 390 nm correspondingly increased with the introduction of TC. A linear relationship A390 = 0.02156 CTC + 0.002122 (R2 = 0.999) was achieved with the wide concentration range from 0.03 μM to 40.00 μM, and the LOD was 3.74 nM. The mutual correction of fluorescence ratiometric and colorimetric methods not only improved the accuracy of detection but also supplemented the detection range. The European Union and China prescribes that the acceptable residue level for TC is 1.35 μM in pork kidney, 0.225 μM in eggs and 0.225 μM in milk [20,35,45,46]. This sensor had wide detection ranges including the maximum residual limit of relevant standards for actual samples, and its LODs were lower than the maximum residual limit of relevant standards, making it practical and feasible in TC detection. As depicted in Fig. 3c, the CIE changed from (0.16, 0.12) to (0.24, 0.17), which consisted with the phenomenon of the color transition of Eu-L-CD+TC from blue to red region.

To further elucidate the advantages of Eu-L-CDs in the detection of TC, the optical properties of Eu-L-CDs and the undoped L-CDs were compared (Fig. S13-S14). The result demonstrated that Eu-L-CDs possessed stronger fluorescence intensity, significant color changes, longer fluorescence lifetime (1.522 ns than 0.422 ns), higher FLQY (8.35 % than 6.28 %) and lower LOD (2.82 nM than 3.07 µM). To confirm the advantages of this colorimetry for TC, the pure TC sensitivity was investigated and compared with the colorimetric methods of this sensor (Fig. S15). The linear relationship for pure TC was A360 = 0.0145 CTC + 0.00942 (R2 = 0.998) in the range of 0.20–40.00 μM, with a detection limit of 0.185 μM. Therefore, Eu-L-CDs had wider detection range and lower detection limit than those of pure TC, indicating that the sensor as colorimetric method had great advantages in detecting TC. Additionally, Eu-L-CDs exhibited better response to TC than achiral CDs, indicating that the Eu-L-CDs could achieve the sensitive and specific detection of TC. Compared with other probes for detecting TC (Table S5), the sensor combined unique chirality of CDs with ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric methods and showed lower LODs. In addition, the actual sample detection of Eu-L-CDs ratiometric fluorescence sensor included both solid and liquid samples, which provided a more reliable application potential for detecting TC residues. Therefore, these results demonstrated the excellent ability of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor for the highly sensitive and accurate detection of TC.

At present, the actual samples of most detection methods for TC are mainly liquid samples, lacking measurement in solid animal-derived food [47]. Therefore, the current sensor evaluated the detection of TC in both liquid and solid actual samples (including pork kidney, eggs and milk) through ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric manners. The initial concentration of TC in the preprocessed actual samples were obtained by the sensor. Subsequently, the actual samples recovery was gotten by adding different concentrations of TC standard solutions (1.0, 2.5, and 10.0 μM) to the pretreated samples [48]. The actual samples recoveries of the ratiometric fluorescence method and colorimetric method ranged from 97.96 % to 102.62 % (RSD<3.06 %) and 97.96–102.62 % (RSD<4.95 %), respectively ( Table 1 and S1). In addition, filter paper strips and hydrogels were also employed to evaluate the detection performance of actual samples, and the recovery ranges were 97.41–100.39 (RSD<4.98 %) and 97.98–105.62 (RSD<5.41 %), respectively (Table S2 and S3). The preferable recovery rate of actual samples demonstrated that the sensor possessed excellent accuracy and practicality in detecting TC for actual samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity and anti-interference of the sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To estimate the specific recognition response and anti-interference ability of the Eu-L-CDs ratiometric fluorescence sensor for TC in complex biological environments, representative antibiotics, common inorganic ions and a variety of amino acids were employed. When Eu-L-CDs and Eu-L-CDs+TC solutions were incubated with 100 μM of various interfering substances, no fluctuations of F618/F410 were observed ( Fig. 4). The reason was that the β-diketone structure of TC could strongly coordinate with Eu3+ and transfer energy through AE, causing Eu-L-CDs to emit red fluorescence [1]. Additionally, the fluorescence emission intensity of Eu3+ in the Eu-L-CDs+TC system was significantly higher than that of the Eu-TC complex (Fig. S17), therefore free Eu3+ could not interfere the detection of TC. Furthermore, rare earth ions are trace in nature and actual samples (including pig kidneys, eggs, and milk), so their interference can be ignored. Satisfactorily, the TC family antibiotics hardly interfered with the fluorescence of the sensor, and the reason was explained as follow. The triplet states (T1) of TC, DOX, OTC, CTC measured by phosphorescence spectroscopy were 21600 cm−1, 21830 cm−1, 21880 cm−1 and 22000 cm−1, respectively. In comparison to other TCs, TC exhibited the superior ISC efficiency to sensitize Eu3+ fluorescence emission through AE due to the minimal T1 state [49,50]. As presented in Fig. 4a, Al3+ produced the false positive signal, which could be effectively shielded by the masking agent triethanolamine. Overall, the current sensor displayed fantastic specificity and anti-interference for the TC detection.

The fluorescence spectra, UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence lifetime of Eu-L-CDs were measured to clarify the fluorescence quenching mechanism. The fluorescence excitation spectrum of Eu-L-CDs overlapped considerably with the UV–vis absorption spectrum of TC, indicating that the fluorescence decreasing of Eu-L-CDs by TC might be relative to the IFE mechanism (Fig. S16). The Parker equation was employed to further confirm the mechanism.
(1)
F
cor
F
obsd
=
2.3 dA
ex

10

A

g

2.3 s
A

− s
A
em

The absorbance values (Aex and Aem) of Eu-L-CDs at 325 nm and 410 nm, the width of the cuvette (d), the distance between the excitation beam edges (g), the cuvette edge and the width of the excitation beam (s) were calculated to obtain the corrected and measured fluorescence intensities ratio (Fcor/Fobsd). When the concentration of the introduced TC increased from 0.0 μM to 10.0 μM, the Fcor/Fobsd value raised from 1.338 to 1.684, demonstrating the quench mechanism was consistent with IFE effect [51].

A new absorption peak was observed at 390 nm in the Eu-L-CDs+TC system (Fig. 2c), indicating the formation of new chemical bonds between Eu-L-CDs and TC, so the fluorescence quenching of Eu-L-CDs by TC might be associated with SQE. After introduction of TC, the fluorescence lifetimes of Eu-L-CDs (1.538 ns to 1.374 ns) and undoped L-CDs (0.422 ns to 0.334 ns) almost unchanged, which further supported the possible quenching process of IFE and SQE (Fig. 2d, S13e). In addition, the Stern-Volmer equation was utilized to verify the fluorescence quenching mechanism of SQE.
(2) F0/F=1+ Kqτ0 [Q]

The fluorescence intensity (F and F0) in the presence and absence of TC ([Q]), and the fluorescence lifetime of Eu-L-CDs (τ0) were employed to calculate the quenching rate constant (Kq). The obtained Kq was 4.186×1013 M−1 s−1, larger than the dynamic quenching rate constant of 1.0×1010 M−1s−1, further confirming the occurrence of SQE [43].

The red fluorescence emission of free Eu3+ at 618 nm was decreased in the existence of H2O. The fluorescence emission intensity of Eu3+ in Eu-L-CDs+TC system was significantly higher than that of Eu-TC complex (Fig. S17), indicating L-CDs could effectively remove the coordinated water molecules of Eu3+ [52]. To further manifest the quenching effect of H2O on Eu3+, the fluorescence intensity and lifetime changes of Eu3+ at 618 nm in H2O and D2O solutions before and after the introduction of TC were examined. In both the Eu3++TC and Eu-L-CDs+TC systems, the emission intensity of Eu3+ in D2O solution obviously increased in comparison with that in H2O solution (Fig. S17). In addition, the quantity of H2O (nw) coordinated with Eu3+ and Eu-L-CDs in the presence of TC was calculated by Horrocks and Supkowski equation [48]:
(3) nw = 1.1 (τH2O −1-τD2O −1-0.31)

The fluorescence lifetimes of Eu3+ in H2O (τH2O) and D2O (τD2O) solutions were calculated (Table S4), the nw in the Eu3++TC and Eu-L-CDs+TC system was obtained to be 1.437 and 0.132, respectively. The calculation results indicated that the coordination H2O of Eu3+ caused a significant quenching effect, and the substitution of L-CDs and TC for the coordinated H2O led to increasing to Eu3+ emission. The reason was that the β-diketone structure of TC could coordinate with Eu3+ to form new bonds and transfer energy through AE to make Eu3+ emit red fluorescence [50].

The Eu-L-CDs+TC system exhibited two fluorescence peaks of Eu3+ at 590 nm and 618 nm (Fig. S17), which originated from 5D0→7 F1 and 5D0→7 F2 transitions of Eu3+ [53], respectively. The hypersensitive transition of 5D0→7 F2 was stronger, thus the peak of Eu3+ at 618 nm was combined with the emission of L-CDs at 410 nm to form a ratiometric sensor. Furthermore, the fluorescence lifetime of Eu-L-CDs at 618 nm increased from 0.87 ns to 0.93 ns when the concentration of TC added to Eu-L-CDs increased from 5.0 μM to 10.0 μM, demonstrating the fluorescence intensity enhancement of Eu3+ in the current sensor (Fig. 2e).

As described in Fig. 2f, the energy of singlet state (S1) and triplet state (T1) of TC was measured to be 27777.8 cm−1 and 21600.0 cm−1, respectively. The energy difference between S1 and T1 of TC was 6178.0 cm−1 (>5000.0 cm−1), and according to the Reinhoudt criterion, intersystem crossing (ISC) between S1 and T1 could occur [43]. The 5D0 state of Eu3+ was determined to be 17260.0 cm−1 [48], which was located below the triplet state (T1) of TC. According to Dexter's sensitization mechanism, the energy transfer from the triplet state (T1) of TC to the 5D0 state of Eu3+ could enhance the emission of Eu3+. Therefore, this sensor possessed excellent sensitivity and absolute advantage for TC detection.

Due to cumbersome operations and large instruments, actual sample testing on site is severely limited. Therefore, smartphone assisted solid sensors were designed for rapid and portable on-site analysis of TC. Due to the wonderful dispersion and optical features under complicated conditions, these Eu-L-CDs could be evenly scattered in the test strips and hydrogel microspheres solid sensors. Upon adding different concentrations of TC, the sensors displayed visible color change from blue to red under 365 nm UV–vis lamp ( Fig. 5). According to Color Picker software of smartphone, the ratio of red channel (R) to blue channel (B) displayed an excellent linear relationship, with R/B = 0.114 CTC + 0.409 (R2 = 0.995) for test strips and R/B = 0.0957 CTC + 0.125 (R2 = 0.998) for hydrogel microspheres, respectively. The LODs (20.81 nM and 29.89 nM) were below the residual limit standard, confirming that solid sensors had great potential for on-site detection of TC. In summary, Eu-L-CDs could retain excellent performance in the solid sensors, which could serve as a wonderful implement for portable and visual detection of TC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adjuvants screening for TC resistant bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial antibiotic resistance has raised scientific warnings about difficult treatment infections and high mortality rates. Therefore, screening effective antibiotic adjuvants is expected to become a new approach to make bacteria re-sensitive to drugs. The ratiometric fluorescence sensor was employed to screen TC adjuvants by a simple program ( Fig. 6a). Firstly, the bacteria were incubated together with different adjuvants and TC. Subsequently, the concentration of TC in bacteria was quantitatively analyzed by the sensor. At the concentration of TC to effectively inhibit Escherichia coli (400 μM) (Fig. 6b), TC content in the bacteria was 0.642 μM, which was determined as the critical value for subsequently screening TC adjuvants drugs. If the TC content in bacteria exceeded the critical value, the candidate drug was considered to be effective adjuvants. After incubating Escherichia coli with MET (50 mM) and TC (100 μM), the content of TC in bacteria increased to 0.700 μM and exceeded the critical value (Fig. 6c), indicating that MET could serve as an effective adjuvants for TC [33]. However, when Escherichia coli was co-incubated with Glu (50 mM) and TC (100 μM), the concentration of TC in bacteria was just 0.330 μM, manifesting that Glu could not facilitate the uptake of bacteria toward TC. The screening process of effective TC adjuvants though the ratiometric fluorescence strategy could be completed within approximate 3 h.

In conclusion, a novel ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on chiral Eu-L/D-CDs was designed for highly selective and portable detection of TC in animal-derived food by reducing the blue fluorescence through IFE and SQE and enhancing the red fluorescence of Eu3+ through AE. The low detection limit was under the national residue standards, which excellently satisfied the TC detection requirements. Importantly, the Eu-L-CDs possessed excellent optical properties and dispersity, thus exhibited extensive potential to prepare portable solid-state sensors for visual analysis of TC. Additionally, the sensor facilitated to rapidly screen of TC adjuvants in bacteria, laying an extraordinary foundation for the future development to combat antibiotic resistance. Therefore, this sensor not only achieved the specific and portable detection of TC in animal-derived food but also provided new perspective for expanding the development and application of CDs.

Liu Yibing: Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis. Sun Guoying: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Investigation. Yin Xiangyu: Investigation. Su Kaihua: Software. Hu Kaixin: Project administration. Song Xuewei: Visualization. Liu Min: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Data curation. Xie Yuxin: Investigation. Wei Shanshan: Visualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Jilin Province Natural Science Foundation (20220101350JC).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2024.136753.

Inorg. Chem.

Ind. Eng. Chem. Res.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Min Liu is a master student in Analytical Chemistry at Changchun University of Technology. Her research topic is the preparation and application of novel ratiometric fluorescent sensors based on chiral carbon dots in analytical detection and sensing.

Guoying Sun has received PhD degree from the Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, and subsequently has pursued postdoctoral research at Northeast Normal University. Currently, she is a professor in the Department of Chemistry at Changchun University of Technology. At present, her research interests are focused on the application of nanomaterial fluorescence in analytical chemistry and optical sensor technology.
